author: @myblueshades
text:
Gross generalisations that may help you 'home in' on best practice.
30A per mm of rod, more or less- So 2.5mm @80A, 3.2mm @100A. 
The arc length 'home' is the same as the rod diameter, a larger rod should have a longer arc. 
Maintaining a shorter arc on thin material helps with burn through, likewise a longer arc will provide a hotter puddle to increase penetration on thicker parent metals.
A poor earth is like a kinked hosepipe. No amount of swearing at the tap will increase the flow :)

Most important top tip.
Develop a slight twisting action as you strike the arc. Sticking will become a thing that happens to other people.

Also, milk mitigates zinc ingestion

author: @ronprince1478
text: Fancy that, learning by doing 🤔. Pain is a very good teacher (hot copper looks like cold copper). Protecting yourself when your young is not important until your not young. Emphysema (spray painting without protection (even airbrushing)), hearing protection heavy machinery, hammering and a multitude of other times (I now need hearing aids). I have had welding flash in both eyes at the same time (another welder I should have been paying more attention to) (feels like hot sand in your eyes for two days), it’s a bugger when you can’t find your beer on the table in front of you. I’m 65 now and the things I didn’t do when I was young are a little more important. Would I do it all again? In a heartbeat, but with just a few small changes, slow down just a little and think about later on in life, the pain (price) doesn’t always come at the start. Doing and making has a huge reward, it doesn’t matter if it’s a work bench or a work of art. Doing and failing (the first time) is learning, not doing (because of fear of failing) is nothing, especially not learning. I absolutely love this channel, getting to learn new things and laugh out loud is brilliant. THANKYOU

author: @jamesross1003
text: Ok here is my welding tip, don't try to wipe sweat off of your forehead with your welding hand while it is energized.  I didn't do this of course, but in welding class in high school a boy did just that.  It arced to the metal frame around the view port and melted into the helmet(basically the rod burned through his helmet).  Talk about a blood curdling scream.  So it's best just not to get near your helmet with your hot rod or any body part for that matter.  In other words if you don't want to stick it, don't lick it.  That came from my welding teacher and is great advice.

author: @MrHunterseeker
text: Welding.  I got state certified in high school, took 2 years of welding classes and at the end of the classes, tested on MiG, Stick and Tig welders, including brazing. The most important things to remember when welding- CLEAN and PREPARE for the welds.  Clean all  grease/oil or other contaminants off surfaces, but also, any paint that would be in the way. Best use a degreaser, then come back after you degrease, wipe dry then use a grinder and grind all mill scale off area or rust, or paint. Use grinder, cut a bevel shape V into the two pieces of metal you are trying to fuse together. Do not use fancy movements. Keep it Simple Stupid (KISS) Use gravity to your benefit. Keep your stinger/welding rod/gun at 45 degrees and slowly drag the rod/stiger/gun from left to right on flat and overhead welds. (Yes overhead welds are harder than flat).  Vertical welds and Tig Welds are the only welds that require fancy movements. When you do a true vertical weld, to do it properly you should hold the stinger in front of you, and let your wrist do all the work, doing backwards Z motions until the rod is used up. TiG is the same way, you "walk the cup".  Things to remember, if it's cast, then you might as well buy new because, even though I have seen people weld cast before, by preheating, it never lasts. It always ends up failing. If you are in a windy area or have a shop fan blowing in your direction, be sure to keep your stinger out of the wind, either set up some welding curtains or block the airflow off your welds.  The numbers on rods tell you what they are made for. First two numberstell you the psi the rod is rated for,  70 means 70,000 psi, the 3rd number tells you what positions you can use the rod in, and the final number tells you the material the filler rod is made from.

author: @geraldelwood9660
text:
Please don't give the average Joe out there the confidence to treat electrical equipment with gay abandon just because the voltage does not exceed the standard touch voltage of 50V AC.
Firstly the input voltage is of course 230V and faults within the equipment could result in 230V being present on the metal casing. But even apart from faults in the equipment; there could be faults in the person using it.  i.e.  touch voltage of 50V is based on a normal person in dry conditions with no blemishes on their body and a healthy heart. If they have recent scars or lesions in their skin or even very sweaty skin at the point of contact, the initial insulating layer (skin) is drastically compromised and this would allow a greater flow of current into the body. As I'm sure you know, a welding device provides enough current to kill over 1000 people at the same time. It is only the reduced voltage and the resistance of the combined parts of the body which make the danger considered to be of an acceptable level. The combination of complacency and limited knowledge has always been a danger in many parts of life and this is certainly true when using electricity. 
For those who are unaware; it only takes a few thousandths of an Amp to send someone's heart into ventricular fibrillation.  The common protective devices in domestic fuse boards are set to trip at only  15 to 30 thousandths of an Amp.
I say all this not to scare people from using such devices, but to ask them to be respectful of the potential for danger from accident or misuse when using them.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the input voltage of everything in the UK including toasters and kettles is 230V - should I advise folks not to make a cup of tea or have toast for breakfast? What you are saying is far too nanny - I would just ignore you. Try saying it in a better way

replies.author: @geraldelwood9660
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I'm sure I agree with you that there is far too much "Nanny State" in modern life, and I enjoy your channel. But as an electrician I witness far too much complacency with equipment which is perfectly safe when used as intended but becomes dangerous when used in the wrong way.
To take your example of making breakfast toast - you and I would of course never do this, but how often have you see someone poking a knife into a toaster to dig out a stuck piece of toast. All I wanted to say was "please don't encourage people to switch off their brain, because everything is super safe." But I take your point that I could have expressed myself better.  Keep up the good work 😀

author: @jonnporter6081
text: Great video. A couple of caveats:  If you're going to do a lot of welding, protect any exposed skin from the UV light given off from the process. Anybody standing around, simply closing your eyes does NOT protect your eyes from the UV light. Also, the light reflected off of the walls, ceiling, etc. is just as dangerous as the light directly from the welding itself. You mentioned zinc. Most young people here in America don't know that galvanized coatings contain zinc. Do not weld on galvanized material , unless you're in a WELL ventilated space. The smoke coming from the galvanized coating WILL damage your lungs. I inhaled just a small amount when I was 16 years old. I could barely breathe for a week. My lungs are fine now. But it could have been worse. I won't weld on galvanized material under any conditions, now. Again, great video.

E. 60. 1. 3
Ignore the E
60 is 60,000 psi tensile strength which is common steal strength
1 means you can weld in any position. 2  in the same spot means flat welding only

Seeing the weld puddle so you can work with it is very important. There is a adjustment on the lens to set it for your eyes😁😁

author: @thedevilinthecircuit
text: A good way to set your starting amperage on the welder is to use a digital caliper on the bare end of the welding rod.  Example: a rod with a diameter of .125" uses 125 amps to begin.  Run a bead and see how it's working.  If it's too spitty and blobby, crank up the amps a little.  If it's blowing through the work, back off the amps.

author: @jb-dn3ct
text: Great info for beginners, yes the light will burn your skin, the hot stuff should be treated as such, the fumes, mild steel is not much of a worry, stainless gives off nickel and chromium, aluminium gives of ozone so at this point an air feed should be used although a well ventilated area with fume extraction is all I use and I've never had any problems to date, good lighting of the work piece will help with the initial starting, just avoid having the light coming from behind the mask as it will make it harder to see, good preparation leads to good welding, I prefer to use stone disc for grinding the work down then wire brush wheel to make sure no contamination is left, especially on stainless, avoid using the same discs on different metals as you'll cross contaminate, there is so much to learn with welding but I would recommend anyone to give it a go, it's fun, incredibly useful and best of all it'll save you a fortune and earn you some if you can get on with it

text: Main 2 probs I had when I started welding was worrying about eye damage and heat warping of metal being welded. First prob is helped by getting a weldoing helmet with a light sensitive switching LCD filter. The  2nd prob is helped by changing position along welds in piecemeal fashion to allow cooling and - by taking your time. Obviously thicker metal won't be such a problem.

author: @NEW_INSITE
text: This comment is a little different than most. I'm going to tell you an alternative cheap welder you can make. When I was around 10 years old I was watching my grandfather weld and he showed me his welder. It was a five gallon bucket of salt water. He took a basic 5 gallon plastic paint bucket filled with water to about three or four inches from the top put in four or five cups of salt mix it well, got two pieces of wood about a foot and a half long each, and ran a wire, approximately 12 gauge, maybe 10 gauge would be better. The wire ran from one of the pieces of wood which straddle the top of the bucket near the side, drop down into the water close to the bottom, Maybe 4 inches up from the bottom of the bucket. And attached to the end of The Wire was a piece of metal. He used a fairly thick heavy Nut and Bolt that he hooked the wire to tighten the nut down on it and dropped it into the water. The other piece of wood that straddle the top of the bucket he placed as far away from the first piece of wood as he could and did the same thing with a wire drop down to a bolt or a piece of metal the same as the first one. Then he got an extension cord which he plugged into an outlet, the extension cord should be 10 or 12 gauge wire also, it can be stranded wire. On an outlet you have a polarized plug on the wall. The smaller slot on the right hand side is the one that has 110 volts, here in america. On the left side you have a neutral which is basically a ground electrically speaking. He ran the hot wire, or the one with 110 volts to the first piece of wood and connected it to the wire that dropped down into the water to the bolt. The neutral wire on the extension cord he hooked up to a metal clamp, which acted as the ground that he hooked to his work piece of metal he was going to weld on. Then he took another wire and ran it off of the second piece of wood and connected to the wire that dropped down to the bolt and ran this wire out to a welding handle, the type that you clip your welding rod into. Believe it or not but he did all his welding with this setup. He completely constructed a camper on the back of his truck, built and installed camper jacks on the side of the truck and many other things with this welding arrangement. You can adjust the amperage or the spark or intensity of the weld by moving the two wooden pieces closer together or farther apart depending on how strong or weak you want your amperage to be. Do not let the two bolts to touch. The electricity must go thru the saltwater. Now if you're in Europe you got to remember that you've got 220 volts coming out of your outlet. You would have to take a ground which is I believe the central of three prongs or a copper rod in the ground or a water pipe to get your ground. Either one of the other slots or holes on your plug would give you 110 volts. If you're not sure what I'm talking about do not attempt this. Keep in mind that you must be very careful not to touch the water in the bucket when this is plugged in and active, you could electrocute yourself. Mr Robert Murrey-Smith, I would love for you to perform this experiment on your channel to show people that it actually works. I have built one myself 15 years ago and it works just fine. You may have to adjust the salt in your bucket to a little more or a little less, with experimentation you will get the salinity just right. Seawater is very close to being right. Now being that this is not an electrically approved welder, and it has 110 volts going through it and not voltage under 50 volts, it is not safe if you do not know what you are doing. I do not recommend anybody do this. I must say this as a disclaimer, people use your own discretion in the world, and everybody please be safe. Again I do not recommend anybody try this, I must say that. Ps, my grandfather worked at the Battelle nuclear power plant most of his life in Eastern Washington. He was a very educated man and I respected him very much. God bless everyone.

author: @Spedley_2142
text:
I had an auto sensing helmet when I was learning to weld. It went dark really quickly when I made the spark but because I was learning it started going clear by the time I got the next spark going and then it took ages to go dark again (well, much slower anyway).
It didn't take long to get painful eyes so I used an old normal helmet until I got the hang of it.

replies.author: @campbellpaul
replies.text: As a professional welder, I never used auto-darkening shade helmets and saved my corneas from harm.  The reason is nothing is faster than the speed of light, and I only needed to meet one blind welder to realize only you can care about you.  I learned early to weld with only 11 and 12 shade gold plexiglass lenses and never had any eye strain because of it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mine has setting on it for darkness and activation time - I played with those

replies.author: @Nath8737
replies.text: The uv and infrared rays that cause arc eye are filtered by an auto darkening helmet whether the shading is activated or not. In that regard they are safer than a helmet you need to flip down before striking an arc. If you have a cheap helmet that’s not activating correctly that’s obviously not great but you won’t end up in the ER at 2am with the sand in your eyes pain. But having said that don’t cheap out on any kind of PPE for your eyes or ears you only get one set. My arc welding improved leaps and bounds when I bought a good auto darkening helmet.

author: @K.P.Alexander
text:
Great video! He's right - if you're just starting off, do some research, but don't worry about the minutia (DCEN vs DCEP etc). Remember, you're not gonna be the best at first. But, that's what practice is for. 
Just make sure you take sensible safety precautions as mentioned in the video - including clothing that covers all your skin. That UV light from the arc doesn't fool around.
Also, you may think "I don't need a frivolous auto-darkening helmet". But, trust me, get one.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: Agreed. Great video. Lot's of good info there. Another couple things... you will want to use a certain type of clothing... typically all cotton, with leather protectors. Avoid Synthetic textiles like polyester and nylon because the welding spatter will melt right thru the synthetic fabrics and burn thru into your skin. Make sure your feet are well protected from falling metal. You will also want good ventilation.

replies.text: @Peter, Xcort  MMA 300E   (that's what I heard him say)   No idea what all that means🤷🏼‍♂️.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: MMA is the type of welder mate - it stands for manual metal arc - so it's just an arc welder or stick welder as it is often called. Xcort is the makers name and 300E is the model number
